lessons.title: What Noise Is, and Signal-to-Noise Ratio
lessons.content:
Tune a radio between stations and you hear a steady hiss. Look at a photo taken in a dark room and you see grainy speckle. Listen to a weak phone call and you hear static under the voice. That background mess has a name: **noise**.

**Noise is the small, random electrical wiggle that is always present in every circuit.** Nothing is perfectly still: tiny charges jitter around on their own, and that jitter shows up as a faint, random voltage. You can never fully remove it. It sets a **floor** — a lowest level below which a tiny signal simply gets lost in the hiss, like a whisper drowned out by a noisy room.

The thing you actually want to measure or send — a voice, a sensor reading, a radio transmission — is the **signal**. What matters is not how loud the noise is on its own, but how the signal compares to it. That comparison is the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**: how big the signal is compared with the noise.

- **High SNR** → signal towers over the hiss → clean and easy to read.
- **Low SNR** → signal is about the same size as the hiss → buried and hard to recover.

SNR is usually written as a ratio of *powers* (signal power divided by noise power). It is often given in **decibels (dB)**, a compressed way of writing big ratios; bigger dB means a cleaner signal.

**Recap:** Noise is the ever-present random hiss in a circuit; it sets the floor below which small signals are lost. SNR measures the signal against that floor, and raising SNR is the whole game in low-noise design.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is electronic noise?
choices: A small, random electrical fluctuation that is always present in a circuit
A loud humming caused only by a broken component
The intended signal a circuit is trying to process
Interference that only appears when a circuit is overheating
items.explanation: Noise is the faint, random wiggle present in every real circuit, like the hiss between radio stations. It is not a fault (it is always there), it is not the wanted signal, and it does not require overheating — it exists even in a healthy, cool circuit.

items.prompt: Which everyday example is a good picture of electronic noise?
choices: The steady hiss you hear when a radio is tuned between stations
A song playing clearly on your favorite station
The volume knob being turned all the way down
A radio that is switched completely off
items.explanation: The between-stations hiss is the receiver's own random noise with no strong signal to cover it — a perfect everyday picture. A clear song is signal, a turned-down knob is just low volume, and an off radio produces nothing at all.

items.prompt: Why is noise often called a “floor”?
choices: It is a lowest level below which a tiny signal gets lost in the random hiss
It only appears at the bottom of a circuit board
It is the maximum signal a circuit can handle
It is the ground connection of the circuit
items.explanation: Noise sets the bottom limit of what you can detect: a signal weaker than the noise is buried and cannot be picked out, like a whisper lost in a noisy room. It is not a physical location, a maximum, or the ground node.

items.prompt: What does signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) measure?
choices: How big the signal is compared with the noise
The total power supplied to the circuit
How fast a signal changes over time
The temperature of the circuit
items.explanation: SNR compares the wanted signal against the background noise. It says nothing directly about supply power, signal speed, or temperature — it is purely the size of the signal relative to the hiss.

items.prompt: A clean, easy-to-read signal corresponds to which condition?
choices: High SNR — the signal towers over the noise
Low SNR — the signal is about the same size as the noise
Zero SNR — there is no signal at all
Negative noise — the noise has been removed
items.explanation: When the signal is much larger than the noise, SNR is high and the signal is clear. Low SNR means signal and noise are comparable, so the signal is hard to read. Noise can never be fully removed, so “negative noise” is not a real condition.

items.prompt: A grainy photo taken in a dark room is mostly a problem of:
choices: Low SNR — the light signal is weak, so noise shows up strongly relative to it
Too much signal overwhelming the camera
The camera being broken
Noise being completely absent
items.explanation: In dim light the actual light signal hitting the sensor is small, so the sensor's ever-present noise is large compared with it — low SNR, seen as grain. The camera is working fine; there simply isn't enough signal to bury the noise.

items.prompt: Why can't you simply remove all noise from a circuit?
choices: Random jitter of charges is always present, so some noise is unavoidable
Engineers haven't yet invented a good enough filter
Noise only exists in cheap components
Noise disappears as soon as you add an amplifier
items.explanation: Noise comes from fundamental random motion of charge that is present in any real material above absolute zero, so it can be reduced but never fully eliminated. It is not a matter of missing technology, low-cost parts, or amplifiers (which actually add their own noise).

items.prompt: Two systems carry the same weak signal. System A has SNR of 30 dB; system B has SNR of 5 dB. Which is cleaner?
choices: System A — higher SNR means the signal stands out more above the noise
System B — lower SNR is always cleaner
They are identical because the signal is the same
Neither; dB has nothing to do with cleanliness
items.explanation: Higher SNR (more dB) means the signal is larger relative to the noise, so system A is cleaner. The signal being equal does not matter — what counts is how it compares with each system's noise floor.

items.prompt: Why does noise matter so much to a circuit designer?
choices: It limits the smallest signal you can reliably detect
It increases the maximum voltage a circuit can output
It makes a circuit run faster
It only affects digital circuits, never analog ones
items.explanation: Because noise sets the floor, it decides the weakest signal you can still pull out cleanly — the heart of sensitive design. It does not raise output range or speed, and it affects analog circuits very much (and digital ones too).

lessons.title: Where the Hiss Comes From: Noise Sources
lessons.content:
Now that we know noise is the random hiss that sets the floor, where does it actually come from? Three sources cover almost everything a beginner meets.

**1. Thermal (Johnson) noise.** Inside any resistor, charges jiggle around purely because the material is warm — the warmer it is, the more they jiggle. That jiggling makes a tiny random voltage across the resistor, present even with the circuit switched off. This is the most universal source: *every resistor makes thermal noise.* Its size is captured by a famous formula for the average (root-mean-square, RMS) noise voltage:

$$v_n = \\sqrt{4kTRB}$$

In words: noise voltage depends on temperature $T$, resistance $R$, the range of frequencies you let through (the **bandwidth** $B$), and a fixed number $k$ (Boltzmann's constant). Bigger $R$, hotter $T$, or wider $B$ → more noise.

**2. Shot noise.** Electric current is really many separate charges arriving one at a time, like raindrops on a roof. Because they arrive at random instants, the count fluctuates slightly — that flicker is shot noise. It grows with the amount of DC current flowing and appears where charges cross a barrier, such as inside a diode or transistor.

**3. Flicker (1/f) noise.** Real devices have a little extra noise that is strongest at **low frequencies** and fades as frequency rises (hence “1/f,” meaning it falls off as frequency $f$ goes up). It depends on how the device was made. It is the troublemaker for slow, near-DC measurements.

A handy way to picture noise is a graph of how much noise sits at each frequency — its **spectral density**. Thermal and shot noise are **“white”**: flat across frequency, the same everywhere. Flicker noise is the rising tail down at low frequency.

**Recap:** Thermal noise (any warm resistor, $v_n=\\sqrt{4kTRB}$), shot noise (random arrival of charges in a current), and flicker/1/f noise (low-frequency, device-dependent) are the three you will meet first.

items.prompt: What physically causes thermal (Johnson) noise in a resistor?
choices: Random jiggling of charges because the material is warm
A large DC current forced through the resistor
Defects from a poor manufacturing process
An external radio station leaking in
items.explanation: Thermal noise comes from the heat-driven random motion of charges, so it exists in any resistor above absolute zero — even with no current and no external interference. Current relates to shot noise, manufacturing relates to flicker noise, and outside stations are interference, a separate thing.

items.prompt: In $v_n = \\sqrt{4kTRB}$, what does the $B$ stand for?
choices: The bandwidth — the range of frequencies allowed through
The battery voltage powering the circuit
The number of resistors in the circuit
The brightness of the signal
items.explanation: $B$ is the bandwidth in hertz; wider bandwidth lets through more noise, so it raises $v_n$. It is not a supply voltage, a component count, or anything about brightness.

items.prompt: According to $v_n = \\sqrt{4kTRB}$, which change increases a resistor's thermal noise voltage?
choices: Raising the temperature, the resistance, or the bandwidth
Cooling the resistor down
Lowering the resistance to zero
Painting the resistor a darker color
items.explanation: Noise grows with $T$, $R$, and $B$ since all three sit inside the square root. Cooling or reducing resistance lowers noise, and color is irrelevant — it has nothing to do with the random motion of charges.

items.prompt: Does thermal noise appear in a resistor even when no current is flowing?
choices: Yes — it depends on temperature, not on any applied current
No — it only appears when current flows
No — it only appears at very high voltages
Yes — but only in broken resistors
items.explanation: Thermal noise is driven by heat, so a warm resistor produces a fluctuating voltage even sitting unpowered on a bench. Needing current describes shot noise instead; thermal noise needs only a temperature above absolute zero, in any healthy resistor.

items.prompt: What is a good everyday picture of shot noise?
choices: Separate raindrops hitting a roof at random instants, so the patter fluctuates
A steady stream of water from a tap
A resistor warming up in the sun
A slow drift in a measurement over hours
items.explanation: Shot noise comes from current being made of discrete charges arriving at random moments, just like individual raindrops making an uneven patter. A perfectly steady stream has no graininess, warming relates to thermal noise, and slow drift relates to flicker noise.

items.prompt: Shot noise tends to grow with:
choices: The amount of DC current flowing across a barrier
The temperature of a resistor
How low the frequency is
The physical size of the circuit board
items.explanation: Shot noise scales with the DC current, since more charges crossing means more random arrivals to count. Temperature drives thermal noise, low-frequency rise is the signature of flicker noise, and board size is unrelated.

items.prompt: What is the defining feature of flicker (1/f) noise?
choices: It is strongest at low frequencies and fades as frequency rises
It is strongest at high frequencies
It is perfectly flat across all frequencies
It only occurs in resistors, never in transistors
items.explanation: Flicker noise rises toward low frequency (the “1/f” shape) and shrinks at higher frequency, which is why it plagues slow, near-DC measurements. Flat-across-frequency describes white noise, and flicker is in fact most associated with active devices like transistors.

items.prompt: Noise that is “white” means:
choices: Its level is flat — about the same at every frequency
It is always positive in voltage
It only occurs in white-colored components
It rises steadily with frequency
items.explanation: By analogy with white light containing all colors equally, white noise has a flat spectral density — equal amounts at every frequency. Thermal and shot noise are white; flicker noise is not, because it rises at low frequency.

items.prompt: A spectral-density graph plots:
choices: How much noise sits at each frequency
How much current flows at each voltage
The resistance versus temperature
The signal amplitude versus time
items.explanation: Spectral density shows noise level as a function of frequency, letting you see white (flat) versus 1/f (rising at low frequency) shapes at a glance. It is not a current-voltage curve, a resistance-temperature plot, or a time-domain waveform.

items.prompt: Which source is the most universal — present in every resistor in every circuit?
choices: Thermal (Johnson) noise
Shot noise
Flicker (1/f) noise
External radio interference
items.explanation: Every resistor at a normal temperature makes thermal noise, with no current or special device needed, so it is the most universal source. Shot noise needs current across a barrier, flicker needs an active/imperfect device, and interference comes from outside.

lessons.title: How Noise Adds, and Why Less Bandwidth Means Less Noise
lessons.content:
A real circuit has several noise sources at once — the resistor, the transistor, the next stage. How do their hisses combine? Not the way you might guess.

**Random noises do not simply add up.** Because each source wiggles independently — sometimes up while another is down — they partly cancel moment to moment. The right way to combine independent (“**uncorrelated**”) noises is to **add in quadrature**: square each one, add the squares, then take the square root.

$$v_{total} = \\sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \\cdots}$$

Example: a $3\,\mu V$ source and a $4\,\mu V$ source give $\\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=\\sqrt{25}=5\,\mu V$, not $7\,\mu V$. (“RMS”, root-mean-square, is just the standard way to state a noise size.)

A powerful consequence: **the biggest source wins.** Squaring exaggerates the largest term, so once one source is a few times bigger than the rest, it sets the total almost by itself. The smart move is to hunt down and shrink the single loudest contributor, not to fuss over the small ones.

**Bandwidth.** Recall thermal noise grew with bandwidth $B$ — the range of frequencies you let through. White noise is spread across all frequencies, so the wider the window you open, the more noise you scoop up. **Narrow the bandwidth and you collect less noise.** As long as your signal still fits through the narrower window, the signal stays put while the noise drops — so the SNR improves. This is why a radio's narrow tuning, or a slow careful measurement, can pull a faint signal out of the hiss.

**Recap:** Independent noises add in quadrature (root of the sum of squares), so the largest one dominates; and because white noise fills every frequency, cutting bandwidth cuts noise and lifts SNR.

items.prompt: How do two independent (uncorrelated) noise sources combine?
choices: In quadrature — square each, add the squares, take the square root
By simple addition of their values
By subtracting the smaller from the larger
By multiplying the two values together
items.explanation: Independent noises partly cancel moment to moment, so they add as the root of the sum of squares, not by plain addition, subtraction, or multiplication. This is what “adding in quadrature” means.

items.prompt: Two uncorrelated sources are 3 microvolts and 4 microvolts RMS. What is the total?
choices: 5 microvolts
7 microvolts
1 microvolt
12 microvolts
items.explanation: Add in quadrature: square root of (3 squared + 4 squared) = square root of 25 = 5 microvolts. Plain addition would wrongly give 7; subtraction gives 1; multiplication gives 12. None of those is how random noise combines.

items.prompt: Why do independent noise sources NOT simply add together?
choices: They wiggle independently, so they partly cancel moment to moment
One source always blocks the other
Noise voltages are always negative
Adding noise is forbidden by Ohm's law
items.explanation: Because the sources are uncorrelated, at any instant one may push up while another pushes down, so they partly cancel — giving a quadrature sum rather than a direct sum. They do not block each other, noise swings both ways, and Ohm's law is unrelated.

items.prompt: One noise source is 10 microvolts and another is 1 microvolt. Roughly what is the quadrature total?
choices: About 10 microvolts — the larger source dominates
Exactly 11 microvolts
About 5.5 microvolts — the average
About 1 microvolt — the smaller dominates
items.explanation: Square root of (100 + 1) = square root of 101, which is about 10.05 microvolts — essentially the big one. Squaring exaggerates the largest term, so a source several times bigger sets the total by itself. The small contributor barely matters.

items.prompt: Given how noises add, what is the smartest way to reduce total noise?
choices: Find and shrink the single largest contributor
Shrink the smallest contributor first
Add more small noise sources to average them out
Increase every source equally
items.explanation: Since the biggest source dominates the quadrature sum, attacking it gives the largest improvement. Trimming small sources barely moves the total, adding sources only increases noise, and raising everything is plainly counterproductive.

items.prompt: Why does opening up a wider bandwidth let in more noise?
choices: White noise is spread across all frequencies, so a wider window scoops up more
Wider bandwidth heats the resistors
Wider bandwidth increases the supply voltage
Wider bandwidth multiplies the signal
items.explanation: White noise sits at every frequency, so the more frequencies you admit, the more total noise you collect. Bandwidth does not change temperature or supply voltage, and it does not amplify the signal.

items.prompt: You narrow a receiver's bandwidth and the signal still fits through. What happens to SNR?
choices: SNR improves — noise drops while the signal is unchanged
SNR gets worse — narrowing always hurts
SNR is unchanged — bandwidth has no effect on noise
The signal disappears entirely
items.explanation: Narrowing the window lets in less noise while the signal, still inside the window, is unchanged — so the ratio of signal to noise rises. Bandwidth strongly affects noise, and as long as the signal fits it is not lost.

items.prompt: Tuning a radio to a narrow band around one station, instead of a wide band, mainly helps because it:
choices: Rejects noise at all the frequencies where the station isn't
Makes the station transmit more power
Cools the radio's circuits
Removes thermal noise from the resistors entirely
items.explanation: A narrow tuning admits only the slice of frequencies the station uses, rejecting the noise everywhere else and raising SNR. It cannot change the transmitter's power, cool the circuit, or fully eliminate thermal noise (which is always present).

items.prompt: Two sources that are perfectly correlated (move exactly together) would add:
choices: By simple addition — they reinforce instead of partly canceling
In quadrature, like uncorrelated sources
To zero, always
By multiplication
items.explanation: Correlated sources rise and fall in step, so they reinforce and add directly. The quadrature rule applies only to independent sources; correlated ones add straight up, which is exactly why correlation makes the combined noise larger.

lessons.title: Noise Figure, the First Stage, and Practical Fixes
lessons.content:
Every amplifier you add to a chain not only boosts the signal — it also adds a bit of its own hiss, making the SNR a little worse on the way out than it was coming in. We measure that damage with the **noise figure**.

**Noise factor and noise figure.** The **noise factor** $F$ is simply how much worse the SNR gets through a stage: the input SNR divided by the output SNR. A perfect, noiseless stage would leave SNR unchanged ($F=1$); every real stage makes it a little worse, so $F$ is greater than 1. Written in decibels, $F$ becomes the **noise figure (NF)**; a perfect stage has $NF = 0\,dB$, and smaller NF is better.

**Which stage matters most?** In a chain of amplifiers, you might expect every stage to add equally. It doesn't work that way. The very **first stage dominates** the total noise. Here is the intuition: the first amplifier boosts the weak input — noise and all — to a healthy level. The later stages add their own hiss, but they add it to an *already-large* signal, so their contribution barely dents the SNR. But the first stage acts on the *tiny raw* signal, where any added hiss does real harm. This idea — that each later stage's noise gets divided down by the gain in front of it — is captured by the **Friis formula** for a cascade.

**The headline rule: put the low-noise amplifier (LNA) first.** Choose a first stage with very low noise and decent gain, and the whole chain inherits its low noise. The classic mistake is putting something lossy — a long cable, a filter — *before* the first amplifier, because that loss directly worsens the front of the chain where it hurts most. (In a radio or radar receiver, the LNA sits right at the antenna for exactly this reason.)

**Practical ways to lower noise:** narrow the bandwidth to what the signal needs; pick low-noise parts and circuit topologies; keep the first stage clean and put the LNA first; and shield and ground carefully so outside interference doesn't add to the floor.

**Recap:** Noise figure measures how much a stage spoils SNR; the first stage dominates, so a low-noise amplifier goes first; and bandwidth, good parts, and shielding/grounding are the everyday levers for a lower noise floor.

items.prompt: What does the noise figure of a stage tell you?
choices: How much the stage worsens the signal-to-noise ratio
How much voltage gain the stage provides
How hot the stage runs
How fast the stage responds
items.explanation: Noise figure measures how much SNR degrades passing through a stage — the SNR damage it causes. It is not a measure of gain, temperature, or speed, though those are separate properties of the stage.

items.prompt: A perfect, noiseless stage would have a noise factor F of:
choices: 1 (a noise figure of 0 dB)
0 (a noise figure of minus infinity)
Greater than 10
Exactly 3 dB
items.explanation: A noiseless stage leaves SNR unchanged, so input SNR equals output SNR and F = 1, which is 0 dB. Real stages always add some noise, making F greater than 1 (NF greater than 0 dB).

items.prompt: In a chain of amplifiers, which stage contributes the most to the total noise?
choices: The first stage
The last stage
The middle stage always
All stages contribute exactly equally
items.explanation: The first stage acts on the tiny raw signal, so any noise it adds does the most damage and dominates the total. Later stages add their hiss to an already-amplified signal, so they matter much less; the contributions are not equal.

items.prompt: Why does noise added by later stages barely hurt the overall SNR?
choices: It is added to a signal the first stage has already amplified to a healthy level
Later stages never produce any noise
Later stages remove noise from the signal
Noise cannot exist after the first amplifier
items.explanation: Once the first stage has boosted the signal, a later stage's hiss is small compared with that large signal, so it dents SNR only slightly. Later stages do make noise; they simply add it where it matters less.

items.prompt: What is the headline design rule that follows from “the first stage dominates”?
choices: Put a low-noise amplifier (LNA) first
Put the noisiest stage first to get it over with
Put a lossy filter first to clean the input
Make every stage identical
items.explanation: Because the first stage sets the noise floor, you give it the lowest noise and decent gain — an LNA goes first. Putting a noisy or lossy element first is exactly the mistake to avoid, and identical stages waste the chance to optimize the front.

items.prompt: Why is putting a long lossy cable BEFORE the first amplifier a classic mistake?
choices: Loss at the very front directly worsens the noise where it hurts most
Loss makes the signal stronger
Cables add gain to the chain
Loss only matters at the output end
items.explanation: A loss ahead of the first amplifier weakens the already-tiny signal before any gain is applied, degrading the front of the chain where noise damage is greatest. Loss weakens, not strengthens, the signal, and a cable provides no gain.

items.prompt: In a radio or radar receiver, why is the LNA placed right at the antenna?
choices: To boost the weak signal with low added noise before any loss can hurt it
To keep the antenna warm
Because the LNA must be the last stage
To increase the transmitter's output power
items.explanation: Placing the LNA at the antenna sets a low-noise, high-gain first stage before cables or filters can degrade the faint signal — the practical form of “LNA first.” It is not about temperature, it is the first (not last) stage, and a receiver doesn't set transmitter power.

items.prompt: Which of these is NOT a sensible way to lower a circuit's noise floor?
choices: Widening the bandwidth far beyond what the signal needs
Narrowing the bandwidth to what the signal needs
Choosing low-noise components and topologies
Shielding and grounding carefully
items.explanation: Widening bandwidth beyond need lets in extra noise and raises the floor — the opposite of what you want. Narrowing bandwidth, picking low-noise parts, and good shielding/grounding all genuinely reduce noise.

items.prompt: How does careful shielding and grounding help with noise?
choices: It keeps outside interference from adding to the noise floor
It cools the components to absolute zero
It removes thermal noise from inside the resistors
It increases the signal bandwidth
items.explanation: Shielding and good grounding block external interference (hum, radio pickup) from getting in, keeping the floor low. They cannot reach absolute zero, cannot remove a resistor's own thermal noise, and do not widen bandwidth.

items.prompt: A first stage has very low noise and high gain. What does the Friis idea say about the stages after it?
choices: Their noise is divided down by the front-end gain, so it barely affects the total
Their noise dominates the total instead
Their noise is added directly with no reduction
They must also be low-noise amplifiers or the chain fails
items.explanation: Friis shows each later stage's noise is effectively divided by the gain ahead of it, so a high-gain, low-noise first stage suppresses the rest. Later stages can be ordinary parts — their noise is reduced, not dominant, and not added at full strength.
